name: Infrastructures of Comic-making
description:
How does the world of comic-making function? This panel discussion focuses on the landscape of comic-making, funding and revenue models for comic art, and what authorship looks like in the times of emerging technologies like AI. Director of CIBDI Vincent Eches, Indian artist Pinaki De and journalist/editor/cultural commentator Sonia Dechamps join this discussion moderated by Karthika VK, Publisher, Westland Books. This session brings to light the contemporary scenarios in both French and Indian perspectives.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Signing Illustrations - ISL Guided Walk
description:
Image Credit: Arun Prasad's Comic Archive
Join us for a special Indian Sign Language-interpreted, Deaf-led guided walk of A Moving Line.

Led by Akanksha Kambale from the MAP team, this session centres on Deaf perspectives, with Indian Sign Language (ISL) as the primary language of engagement, offering a visual-first, inclusive approach to experiencing the exhibition.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Sonic Coins
description:
Sonic Coins, is a listening session that explores the material, historical, and sonic lives of coins from the museum's collection, featuring a sound work created through research and cataloguing, using the textures, clinks, and resonances of the coins to imagine a unique sonic archive.

Spanning histories of trade, cultural exchange, modernity, and colonial rule, the selected coins invite new ways of engaging with objects and their journeys. The session, with Shravani Upadhyay from the MAP team, concludes with a viewing of the featured coins and a conversation about their histories and significance within the collection.

name: Panels, Pages and Presentations
description:
A Moving Line gathers more than 250 works to trace how India has told stories in sequence across fifteen hundred years. Come, witness both the creation of new visual identities and the evolution of a nation through a vast body of works across time and space as Khushi Bansal from the MAP team guides you through it. Follow a single line from a temple wall in Bengaluru to the touchscreen, and watch a country tell its stories.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Stories of Neurodivergence: A Short Film Screenings
description:
Join us for an evening of short film screenings, curated by Deepthi, that bring into focus stories of neurodivergent identity, resilience, and authentic connection. Through intimate and thought-provoking narratives, these films explore the realities of neurodivergent lives and the parents of neurodivergent children.

Spanning diverse parental experiences, the screenings invite audiences to engage with stories that challenge stigma through the power of storytelling. These films offer a window into the complex inner worlds that exist beyond labels, celebrating the creativity and unique perspectives that come with thinking differently and embracing acceptance.

The programme aims to create space for dialogue grounded in understanding, mutual support, and the quiet power of shared experience. Whether you are neurodivergent yourself or seeking to understand better, this evening is a celebration of the struggle, acceptance, and brilliance of neurodivergent individuals and their caregivers/parents.

workPresented.name: Stories of Neurodivergence: A Short Film Screenings

name: Artist-in-Dialogue
description:
Image Credits: Panels from Will to Live by Mia Jose
Comic artist Mia Jose and graphic novelist Simon Lamouret will converse about their experiences and the inspirations that led them to create their works. This session focuses on identity, culture and the city, drawing on their individual practices and the spaces their works are rooted in. The notion of city is rooted in some of their works, which helps us see a space as one’s company, a space of constant changes and as identity. Get to know about their thought processes, artistic inspirations and how the country they grew up in influenced their works. 

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Live Drawing
description:
Image credit: Sonia Dechamps
Join graphic novelist David Prudhomme for a live drawing performance accompanied by a selection of music chosen by the artist. As images unfold in real time, Prudhomme invites audiences into the intimate relationship between sound, memory, and visual storytelling.

The performance will be followed by a talk on his work, with a focus on the role of music in his graphic novels. Music is central to Rébétiko and Rébétissa (Futuropolis), his two-part exploration of the outlawed Greek musical tradition of the 1930s and the histories, communities, and cultural landscapes it emerged from.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling
description:
Join us for the launch of MAP’s latest publication, A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling, is a first of its kind anthology tracing India’s long and rich history of visual storytelling, from temple walls and painted scrolls to comics, graphic novels, and webcomics.

The book launch is followed by a panel discussion with graphic artist and researcher Sarita Sundar, comics archivist, researcher Arun Prasad, publication designer Mira Malhotra and artist Partha Banik, moderated by Saba Nehal from the MAP team.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Ink mattu kathe - A Kannada Guided Walk
description:
Do you remember Shikari Shambu? What about Suppandi? We’ve all read stories of these beloved comic characters but do you wonder how they were created? Who drew them; what stories that came before inspired their creators; Did their designs change? Join us for a guided walk in Kannada led by Niharika Shenoy from the MAP team to wonder about these and more questions. Get a sneak peek into the curatorial perspective and the history of Indian visual storytelling.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Reading Between the Panels: A Curator’s Walkthrough
description:
Image Credit: Panel from Vyasa: The Beginning by Sankha Banerjee
Join us for a guided walkthrough of the A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling as its curator Amruta Patil narrates the stories this country tells through lines. In this exhibition, one can witness both the creation of new visual identities and the evolution of a nation through a vast body of works across time and space. Here, kaavad and pattachitra scroll sitting alongside Amar Chitra Katha and Tinkle. Chacha Chaudhary next to the Phantom, while contemporary graphic novels and web comics continue conversations that began centuries ago. 

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Who Is Responsible for Belonging?
description:
What does it mean to belong? Who is responsible for creating the conditions in which every person can participate and feel valued?

For many autistic individuals and their families, everyday life means navigating systems, spaces and expectations that were not designed with them in mind; leaving the work of adapting, advocating and finding support to fall on families themselves.

Presented as part of MAP’s Disability Pride Month 2026 programming, this conversation brings together Bela Joshi and Sneha Joshi, mother and daughter, and mother and sister of an autistic family member, in a dialogue shaped by lived experience and over a decade of work at Colours Centre for Learning. Across two generations, they trace how the questions families ask, and the support they seek change over time.

As a young mother raising an autistic son when awareness and services were scarce, Bela found little institutional support, and built her own community instead. Years later, Sneha's questions as a sibling were different: about identity, belonging and what it means to grow up alongside disability, with even less language available for them. That process of finding, and often having to build support shaped their understanding of interdependence: the idea that none of us moves through the world unsupported, and that belonging is built by communities learning to rely on one another, not by individuals becoming self-sufficient. It is also what grew into Colours Centre for Learning.

Together, they bring a rare combination to this conversation: two generations, two vantage points inside the same family, and over a decade of shared commitment. Bela and Sneha ask what meaningful support can look like today for autistic individuals and for the families around them. Rather than framing inclusion as something families must continually strive for alone, they invite us to imagine belonging as interdependence in practice: a responsibility shared across communities, institutions and generations.

This programme is presented as part of MAP's Disability Pride Month 2026 programming.

name: Up, Close and Centre: Nataraja
description:
Ever wondered why most museums have Nataraja or Dancing Shiva in their collections? It is a result of multiple phenomena that happened during the 18th century. Art history, modern dance, physics, West’s fascination for Eastern culture and more, parallely played a part.

This month’s edition of Up, Close and Centre will feature Abiraami from the MAP team. She looks at Nataraja from our second permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky, through the MAP Collection.

Up, Close & Centre is a series that allows audiences to engage with art in deeper and more meaningful ways by delving into the context, narratives and makings of one artwork from our collection, chosen by our team members.

name: Listening to Comics
description:
Image credit: Arun Prasad's Comic Archive
You’ve probably seen your favorite comic character on the big screens. For the longest time, comic books have been adapted into movies, television shows or animated series to bring them to life, but have you ever heard your comics?

Join us for a live listening session where comic books come alive through vinyl records from Arun Prasad's Comic Archive. Experience your favorite stories in a whole new way of enjoying comics, as you hear from Arun Prasad about his journey of collecting comics. 

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Botanical Beings
description:
How do the materials we work with shape the stories we tell about ourselves?

In this workshop, artist Riya introduces participants to 'Botanical Beings', beginning with a conversation around her practice, participants will look closely at her work before creating their own works using preserved flowers. Working slowly and intuitively, the session invites participants to engage with the materials through observation, reflection, and play, while exploring making as a practice of presence and care.

Together, participants will consider how the ways we engage with the world can shape what we create, and how the works we make can become reflections of our own ways of seeing and being.

This programme is exclusively for 18–25 year olds.

This session is conducted as part of our engagements for the MAP Youth Collective. The MAP Youth Collective is a community for young people (18-25 year olds) to engage with the arts and culture sector. The collective is for, of and by young people — to explore our shared identities and interests through art.

name: Meet and Greet
description:
Image Credits: Archives of Comics Culture Collective
Meet & Greet the artists whose work is on the walls of the galleries you will scroll through. A good chance to interact with them to understand their journey as an illustrator/writer, ask questions or get an autograph!

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.

name: Tholpavakoothu Lecture-Demonstration
description:
Image Credits: Tholpavakoothu Sangam
Tholpavakoothu is Kerala's ancient ritual leather shadow puppetry.  Artist Vipin Viswanatha Pulavar and his team from Tholpavakoothu Sangam present a lecture-demonstration introducing the history, traditions, cultural significance, techniques, and contemporary relevance of Tholpavakoothu. 

The session includes traditional and contemporary adaptations of the performance, a display of authentic leather puppets, explaining their craftsmanship, materials, symbolism, and the process of making and preserving them. You would also get to experience the traditional style of recitation, including selected verses, narration, and vocal techniques used in Tholpavakoothu performances, alongside an introduction to the traditional musical accompaniment and the role of rhythm, storytelling, and ritual elements in the performance.

This programme is part of the celebration of the opening of A Moving Line: 1500 Years of Indian Visual Storytelling.
